prompt:
You are the reference-retriever subagent for project 'Cech-Cohomology'.
            Archon iteration: 002.
            Project directory: /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology
            Project state directory: /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon

            Slug: stacks-cohomology

            Read /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md for your full instructions.
            Read /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon/CLAUDE.md for project-wide context.

            Your directive (also reproduced below for convenience) is at:
              /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon/logs/iter-002/reference-retriever-stacks-cohomology-directive.md

            DIRECTIVE:
            # Reference Retriever Directive

## Slug
stacks-cohomology

## Topic
Algebraic geometry / sheaf cohomology. I need the Stacks Project "Cohomology" chapter
LaTeX source (`cohomology.tex`) — the general topological/abstract-sheaf cohomology
chapter (NOT "Cohomology of Schemes", which is already local as
`references/stacks-coherent.tex`). Two specific results are needed verbatim:
- the **Čech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis** lemma, tagged in the source as
  `cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis` — the basis-comparison criterion with its three
  conditions (closure under finite intersection of the basis, cofinality of the chosen
  covers, Čech vanishing on the basis ⇒ the Čech-to-cohomology comparison is an
  isomorphism / cohomology vanishes);
- the **presheaf description of higher direct images**, tagged
  `cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images` — $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf
  associated to the presheaf $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.

## What the dispatcher will use this for
I am the blueprint-writer for the Čech-higher-direct-image chapter. Two declaration
blocks cite results that live in the Stacks "Cohomology" chapter, which is not yet in
`references/`. I will open the downloaded `cohomology.tex` and copy the verbatim
statements of `cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis` (for the Čech-to-cohomology
comparison on a basis) and `cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images` (for the
presheaf description of $R^k f_*$) into the chapter's `% SOURCE QUOTE:` blocks. Exact
lemma statements and tags matter — these are anti-hallucination citation anchors.

## Seeds
- Stacks Project GitHub raw TeX:
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/cohomology.tex
- Online tags (for cross-checking the label ↔ tag mapping):
  https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01EO (Čech-to-cohomology on a basis, likely)
  https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01XJ (describe higher direct images, likely)

## Out of scope
- Do NOT re-fetch "Cohomology of Schemes" (`coherent.tex`) — it is already local.
- No paraphrase. Download the real `cohomology.tex`.

## Contents-map depth expected
deep — locate the two named lemmas (`cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis` and
`cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images`) by line number inside the downloaded
`cohomology.tex` so I can jump straight to them. No paraphrase of their content.


            Report: /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon/task_results/reference-retriever-stacks-cohomology.md
            (When invoked as a child of another subagent, your report
            lands at task_results/<parent_slug>/reference-retriever-stacks-cohomology.md
            — the Archon CLI handles the path automatically.)

## Protected by the mathematician (`archon-protected.yaml`)

The mathematician owns everything below; respect every rule. Do NOT set or accept an objective that requires violating one.

**Frozen signatures** (you MAY fill the proof body / close a `sorry`, but never rename / re-type / reorder args / weaken hypotheses):
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean`: `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage`

content: Let me read the necessary instruction files first, then proceed with the task.
I'll start by reading the instruction files and then download the required TeX source.

1	---
2	name: reference-retriever
3	description: Download the ORIGINAL source files (PDF + TeX/LaTeX when available) for papers, books, or online mathematical content (arXiv, journal preprints, online textbooks, Stacks Project, nLab) into references/, and register each with a lightweight pointer + table-of-contents stub. Dispatchable by the plan agent and by blueprint-writers when mathematical content needs a source not yet in references/.
4	write_domain: "references/**"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me whenever a strategic decision or a chapter you are
10	    about to write needs material not already in references/. Calling
11	    me is far cheaper than letting a writer or planner hallucinate
12	    sources or paper a hole with vague prose.
13	
14	  - I fetch the ORIGINAL source FILE (PDF, and TeX/LaTeX source when
15	    it is openly available), not a paraphrase. The companion .md I
16	    write next to it is only a citation pointer + table of contents,
17	    NOT a summary you can quote from. When you cite, open the actual
18	    downloaded source file (e.g. references/<slug>.pdf) and quote it
19	    verbatim — that is the whole point of having me fetch it.
20	
21	  - Plan agent: dispatch me BEFORE writing STRATEGY.md or composing
22	    blueprint-writer directives whenever the iteration touches a
23	    topic not represented in references/summary.md. Treat me as
24	    cheap source preparation, not a last-resort lookup.
25	
26	  - Blueprint-writer: when drafting reveals you need a source you
27	    don't have, dispatch me mid-session and wait for me to return
28	    before writing the affected chapter section. Note that to spawn
29	    me from inside a writer round, the planner must have declared
30	    `references/**` in your --write-domain at writer dispatch time
31	    (see blueprint-writer dispatcher_notes).
32	
33	  - I am NEVER dispatched by read-only subagents (blueprint-reviewer,
34	    lean-auditor, lean-vs-blueprint-checker). Those report what they
35	    see; they do not procure new sources.
36	
37	  - I do NOT fabricate. If the original file genuinely cannot be
38	    located through any legitimate open channel, I report "not found"
39	    rather than guessing content or writing a paraphrase in its place.
40	    Treat my "not found" as authoritative — do not redispatch with
41	    more aggressive prompting; instead reconsider the strategy.
42	
43	  - I download the source under references/<slug>.{pdf,tex,...} and
44	    write ONE pointer file references/<slug>.md registering it, then
45	    add it to references/summary.md. I do not delete or rewrite
46	    existing reference entries.
47	---
48	
49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.
67	
68	## Directive Format
69	
70	```markdown
71	# Reference Retriever Directive
72	
73	## Slug
74	<slug>
75	
76	## Topic
77	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>
78	
79	## What the dispatcher will use this for
80	<one paragraph: which chapter / strategy decision the source will inform, so I can prioritise which sections to map and which formats to chase>
81	
82	## Seeds (optional)
83	- arXiv: 1234.5678
84	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
85	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
86	- URL: https://...
87	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
88	
89	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
90	
91	## Out of scope
92	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
93	
94	## Contents-map depth expected
95	- shallow: top-level table of contents only
96	- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
97	- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
98	```
99	
100	If the directive omits "Contents-map depth expected", default to **medium**.
101	
102	## What you do
103	
104	1. **Read your directive completely.**
105	
106	2. **Locate the source.**
107	   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
108	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
109	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
110	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
111	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
112	     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.
113	
114	3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:
115	
116	   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
117	     ```bash
118	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
119	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
120	     ```
121	     If the e-print is a single `.tex`, name it `references/<slug>.tex` instead. You may unpack the tarball (`tar xzf`) into `references/<slug>-src/` if it helps map the contents — but keep the original archive too.
122	
123	   - **Stacks Project**: fetch the relevant `.tex` chapter verbatim from GitHub raw, so lemma/tag statements are exact (this is the fix for the paraphrase/tag-typo failure mode):
124	     ```bash
125	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>-algebra.tex \
126	       https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/algebra.tex
127	     ```
128	     Record the exact tags the dispatcher needs and confirm each tag's statement against the fetched `.tex`.
129	
130	   - **Journal / book with an open form**: download the open PDF (author homepage, institutional repository, numdam, Project Euclid open, publisher open-access, etc.).
131	
132	   - **General**:
133	     ```bash
134	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf "<url>"
135	     ```
136	     `-f` makes curl fail on HTTP errors instead of silently saving an error page.
137	
138	4. **Verify the download is real**, not a paywall/login/error page wearing a `.pdf` extension:
139	   ```bash
140	   ls -l references/<slug>.pdf
141	   file references/<slug>.pdf      # expect "PDF document", not "HTML document"
142	   ```
143	   A near-empty file, an HTML MIME type, or a "log in to continue" body all mean the fetch FAILED — delete the bad file (`rm references/<slug>.pdf`) and treat that source as not obtained. **Never** rename or keep an unverified download.
144	
145	5. **Write the pointer file** `references/<slug>.md` (format below) in BOTH the success and failure cases:
146	   - **On success:** it points at the real downloaded file(s) and records the exact URL each was retrieved from. It does NOT reproduce the source's content.
147	   - **On failure** (nothing could be downloaded and verified): write the pointer anyway, recording the citation, every URL/channel you tried, and why each failed (paywall, 404, HTML-only, etc.). **Do NOT write the source's mathematical content into it** — a failure pointer carries the attempt log, never a paraphrase or recollection of what the source "probably says."
148	
149	6. **Register the entry** in `references/summary.md`. Append (do not delete existing entries) a one-line index entry referencing the slug.
150	
151	7. **Verify the files are on disk** (`ls -l references/<slug>.*`) before reporting back.
152	
153	## Acquiring paywalled / hard-to-find sources
154	
155	Many sources that look paywalled have a legitimate open copy. Before giving up, try, in order:
156	
157	- **arXiv** for the same title/authors — journal papers very often have an arXiv preprint with identical statements.
158	- **The author's personal / institutional homepage** — search `"<title>" <author> pdf` and `<author> publications`.
159	- **Open repositories**: numdam.org (French journals, seminars — this is where Grothendieck's FGA and Séminaire Bourbaki live), Project Euclid open content, institutional repositories, course pages.
160	- **`filetype:pdf` web searches**: `WebSearch` for `"<exact title>" filetype:pdf`. Some hits open a PDF directly — fetch and verify it.
161	- **Open TeX source**: Stacks Project (GitHub), many lecture notes and books publish LaTeX source openly.
162	
163	**Do NOT** use shadow-library / piracy sites (e.g. Sci-Hub, Library Genesis). Stick to legitimately open copies. If, after these steps, only a paywalled copy exists, fetch whatever open metadata you can (official TOC, abstract) into the pointer file's contents map, mark the source `PAYWALLED — no open copy located`, and say so plainly in your report. Do not paraphrase the body to compensate.
164	
165	## Pointer file format
166	
167	`references/<slug>.md` is an index card, not a summary. Keep it short.
168	
169	### Success — file(s) downloaded and verified
170	
171	```markdown
172	# <Title>
173	
174	## Citation
175	<author(s)>, "<title>", <venue / publisher>, <year>. <DOI / arXiv ID / URL>.
176	
177	## Slug
178	<slug>
179	
180	## Retrieval status
181	RETRIEVED — <date>
182	
183	## Local source files
184	- references/<slug>.pdf — PDF, VERIFIED via `file` — retrieved from <successful URL>
185	- references/<slug>.tex — LaTeX source, VERIFIED via `file` — retrieved from <successful URL>   ← if obtained
186	(List every file you saved and the EXACT URL it came from. These successful links are
187	the most important field — a later planner uses them to re-fetch or double-check.)
188	
189	## Why this source
190	<2-3 sentences: which Archon chapter / strategy decision will use this. Copy from the directive's "What the dispatcher will use this for".>
191	
192	## Contents map
193	<A table of contents / section map pointing INTO the downloaded file, so a planner knows where to look. Use the source's own numbering. NO paraphrase of the mathematics — just locations.>
194	
195	- Ch./§ <n> <title> — p.<page>
196	- Ch./§ <n> <title> — p.<page>   ← relevant to this directive
197	- (deep depth only) Theorem/Lemma <number> — §<n>, p.<page>
198	
199	## Caveats
200	<Things the dispatcher should know: paywalled-but-open-copy-found, unusual conventions, known errata, contested results, edition differences. If none, write "None".>
201	
202	## Quality / provenance
203	<one or two sentences: is this the definitive reference or one of several? Where exactly did the file come from, and how confident are you it is the authoritative version?>
204	```
205	
206	### Failure — nothing could be downloaded and verified
207	
208	Still write the pointer, but it is an **attempt log**, not content. A future planner reading it must immediately see that there is no local source to quote and what was already tried (so they don't burn an iteration repeating it).
209	
210	```markdown
211	# <Title>
212	
213	## Citation
214	<author(s)>, "<title>", <venue / publisher>, <year>. <DOI / arXiv ID / URL>.
215	
216	## Slug
217	<slug>
218	
219	## Retrieval status
220	NOT_RETRIEVED — <date>. No local source file exists. DO NOT cite this as `(read from …)`.
221	
222	## Sources tried
223	- <url 1> — <what happened: 404 / paywall / HTML-only login page / corrupt file>
224	- <url 2> — <…>
225	- <search query used> — <no open copy among results>
226	
227	## Why it matters / what to do next
228	<which chapter/decision needed this, and the dispatcher's options: find another route, pick a different source, or treat the dependent block as gated on retrieval>
229	
230	<!-- NO contents map, NO abstract, NO recalled statements. There is nothing verified to record. -->
231	```
232	
233	## `references/summary.md` registration
234	
235	Append one row to the `## File inventory` table. The template has three columns: `File`, `Description`, `How to read (confirmed working)`. Make the retrieval outcome visible in the index itself, so a planner skimming `summary.md` sees at a glance which sources actually have a local file AND which tool they should use to ingest each one:
236	
237	```markdown
238	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → `<slug>.pdf` / `<slug>.tex` | <one-line topic note> | `Read` (with `pages: "1-5"` if long); fallback `pdftotext <slug>.pdf -` |
239	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → ⚠ NOT RETRIEVED (<short reason>) | <topic note> | n/a — no local file |
240	```
241	
242	For the `How to read` column, record what *actually worked* during your verification step (you opened the file to verify it isn't an HTML-paywall stub — write down the command you used). `Read` for PDFs assumes ``poppler-utils`` is installed (``archon setup`` handles it); if `Read` errors with a missing-`pdftoppm` message, record the exact `pdftotext` (or other) fallback you used. The column is a living log — later agents who ingest the same file should overwrite the entry with a better entry if they find one.
243	
244	If `references/summary.md` doesn't exist, create it with the standard header + the inventory table before appending.
245	
246	## Rules
247	
248	### What you CAN do
249	- Use `WebSearch` and `WebFetch` to discover and inspect sources.
250	- Use `Bash` to download, unpack, and verify source files. **Use `curl` — it is present on every supported platform, so no extra install is needed.** `wget` is an optional fallback only if `curl` is somehow missing (`command -v curl || command -v wget`); never assume `wget` exists. `tar`, `file`, and `ls` are likewise standard.
251	- Write downloaded files and the pointer `.md` under `references/`.
252	- Append to `references/summary.md`.
253	
254	### What you MUST do
255	- **Download the real file and verify it.** A pointer file is only valid once its named source file exists on disk and `file` confirms it is the real document.
256	- **Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when openly available, and keep the PDF too.** TeX gives exact statements; the PDF is the universal fallback.
257	- **Cite exact section / page numbers** in the contents map. A map that just says "the paper discusses X" is useless.
258	- **Quote nothing in the pointer file.** Planners quote from the downloaded source, not from you. Your contents map only tells them where to look.
259	
260	### What you MUST NOT do
261	- **Do NOT fabricate or paraphrase the source's content.** No "I believe the paper proves…", no reconstructed proofs in the `.md`. You fetched the file or you didn't.
262	- **Do NOT use piracy / shadow-library sites.** Legitimate open copies only.
263	- **Do NOT delete or rewrite existing `references/` entries.** Only add new ones. If an existing entry is wrong, report it in "Notes for Dispatcher" — do not modify it.
264	- **Do NOT write outside `references/`.**
265	- **Do NOT spawn child subagents.** You are a leaf.
266	
267	## Report format
268	
269	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/reference-retriever-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
270	
271	```markdown
272	# Reference Retriever Report
273	
274	## Slug
275	<slug>
276	
277	## Status
278	<COMPLETE | NOT_FOUND | PARTIAL>
279	<NOT_FOUND: no legitimate open copy of any source could be downloaded.
280	PARTIAL: some sources/formats obtained, others not (e.g. PDF but no TeX, or paywalled).
281	COMPLETE: all directive items downloaded and verified.>
282	
283	## Sources fetched
284	
285	For each: title, URL(s) used, formats downloaded, verification result, pointer file written.
286	
287	- "Some Paper" — https://arxiv.org/abs/1234.5678 — downloaded `references/some-paper.pdf` (PDF, verified) + `references/some-paper.tar.gz` (TeX source, verified) — pointer `references/some-paper.md`.
288	- "Other Book Ch. 5" — paywalled, no open copy located — TOC-only pointer written, NO source file.
289	
290	## Index updates
291	- `references/summary.md` — appended <N> entries: `some-paper`, ...
292	
293	## Notes for Dispatcher
294	<- any source the directive named that turned out to be wrong / mislocated, with the corrected pointer
295	- any related source you noticed that the dispatcher might want next
296	- any pre-existing references/ entry you noticed was wrong or stale (DO NOT modify — report only)
297	- any source you could only obtain paywalled, so the dispatcher can decide whether to find another route>
298	```
299	
300	## Return value
301	
302	Your final assistant message:
303	
304	- One line: `<slug>: <status> — <N> sources downloaded (<formats>), <M> pointer files written`
305	- The path to your full report.
306

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

580	the complex $\mathcal{I}^\bullet$ is exact in degree $n$. Hence
581	$\Im(\mathcal{I}^{n - 1} \to \mathcal{I}^n) =
582	\Ker(\mathcal{I}^n \to \mathcal{I}^{n + 1})$ as sheaves.
583	Since $\tilde \xi$ is a section of the kernel sheaf over $U$
584	we conclude there exists an open covering $U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$
585	such that $\tilde \xi|_{U_i}$ is the image under $d$ of a section
586	$\xi_i \in \mathcal{I}^{n - 1}(U_i)$. By our definition of the
587	restriction $\xi|_{U_i}$ as corresponding to the class of
588	$\tilde \xi|_{U_i}$ we conclude.
589	\end{proof}
590	
591	\begin{lemma}
592	\label{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
593	Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
594	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
595	The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
596	associated to the presheaves
597	$$
598	V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
599	$$
600	with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
601	There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
602	bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$.
603	\end{lemma}
604	
605	\begin{proof}
606	Let $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet$ be an injective resolution.
607	Then $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is by definition the $i$th cohomology sheaf
608	of the complex
609	$$
610	f_*\mathcal{I}^0 \to f_*\mathcal{I}^1 \to f_*\mathcal{I}^2 \to \ldots
611	$$
612	By definition of the abelian category structure on $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules
613	this cohomology sheaf is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
614	$$
615	V
616	\longmapsto
617	\frac{\Ker(f_*\mathcal{I}^i(V) \to f_*\mathcal{I}^{i + 1}(V))}
618	{\Im(f_*\mathcal{I}^{i - 1}(V) \to f_*\mathcal{I}^i(V))}
619	$$
620	and this is obviously equal to
621	$$
622	\frac{\Ker(\mathcal{I}^i(f^{-1}(V)) \to \mathcal{I}^{i + 1}(f^{-1}(V)))}
623	{\Im(\mathcal{I}^{i - 1}(f^{-1}(V)) \to \mathcal{I}^i(f^{-1}(V)))}
624	$$
625	which is equal to $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})$
626	and we win.
627	\end{proof}
628	
629	\begin{lemma}
630	\label{lemma-localize-higher-direct-images}
631	Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
632	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
633	Let $V \subset Y$ be an open subspace.
634	Denote $g : f^{-1}(V) \to V$ the restriction of $f$.
635	Then we have
636	$$
637	R^pg_*(\mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = (R^pf_*\mathcal{F})|_V
638	$$
639	There is a similar statement for the
640	derived image $Rf_*\mathcal{F}^\bullet$ where $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$
641	is a bounded below complex of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
642	\end{lemma}
643	
644	\begin{proof}
645	First proof. Apply Lemmas \ref{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
646	and \ref{lemma-cohomology-of-open} to see the displayed equality.
647	Second proof. Choose an injective resolution
648	$\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet$
649	and use that $\mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}$
650	is an injective resolution also.
651	\end{proof}
652	
653	\begin{remark}
654	\label{remark-daniel}
655	Here is a different approach to the proofs of
656	Lemmas \ref{lemma-kill-cohomology-class-on-covering} and
657	\ref{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} above.
658	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
659	Let $i_X : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$

1681	}
1682	$$
1683	for any open $U \subset X$. Since $\mathcal{I}$ is injective we
1684	have $H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for $n > 0$ (see
1685	Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-higher-derived-functors}).
1686	By the above we see that $H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})$
1687	is surjective and hence $H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$.
1688	Since $\mathcal{F}$ was an arbitrary $\mathcal{O}_X$-module with
1689	vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology we conclude that also
1690	$H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0$ since $\mathcal{Q}$ is another of these
1691	sheaves (see above). By the long exact sequence this in turn implies
1692	that $H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$. And so on and so forth.
1693	\end{proof}
1694	
1695	\begin{lemma}
1696	\label{lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
1697	(Variant of Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-vanish}.)
1698	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1699	Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis for the topology on $X$.
1700	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1701	Assume there exists a set of open coverings $\text{Cov}$
1702	with the following properties:
1703	\begin{enumerate}
1704	\item For every $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1705	with $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ we have
1706	$U, U_i \in \mathcal{B}$ and every $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}$.
1707	\item For every $U \in \mathcal{B}$ the open coverings of $U$
1708	occurring in $\text{Cov}$ is a cofinal system of open coverings
1709	of $U$.
1710	\item For every $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$ we have
1711	$\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$.
1712	\end{enumerate}
1713	Then $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$ and any $U \in \mathcal{B}$.
1714	\end{lemma}
1715	
1716	\begin{proof}
1717	Let $\mathcal{F}$ and $\text{Cov}$ be as in the lemma.
1718	We will indicate this by saying ``$\mathcal{F}$ has vanishing higher
1719	{\v C}ech cohomology for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$''.
1720	Choose an embedding $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I}$ into an
1721	injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1722	By Lemma \ref{lemma-injective-trivial-cech} $\mathcal{I}$
1723	has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
1724	Let $\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}$
1725	so that we have a short exact sequence
1726	$$
1727	0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0.
1728	$$
1729	By Lemma \ref{lemma-ses-cech-h1} and our assumption (2)
1730	this sequence gives rise to an exact sequence
1731	$$
1732	0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0.
1733	$$
1734	for every $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Hence for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1735	we get a short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes
1736	$$
1737	0 \to
1738	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
1739	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
1740	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0
1741	$$
1742	since each term in the {\v C}ech complex is made up out of a product of
1743	values over elements of $\mathcal{B}$ by assumption (1).
1744	In particular we have a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech cohomology
1745	groups for any open covering $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
1746	This implies that $\mathcal{Q}$ is also an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1747	with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all
1748	$\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
1749	
1750	\medskip\noindent
1751	Next, we look at the long exact cohomology sequence
1752	$$
1753	\xymatrix{
1754	0 \ar[r] &
1755	H^0(U, \mathcal{F}) \ar[r] &
1756	H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \ar[r] &
1757	H^0(U, \mathcal{Q}) \ar[lld] \\
1758	&
1759	H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) \ar[r] &
1760	H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) \ar[r] &
1761	H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) \ar[lld] \\
1762	&
1763	\ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
1764	}
1765	$$
1766	for any $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Since $\mathcal{I}$ is injective we
1767	have $H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for $n > 0$ (see
1768	Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-higher-derived-functors}).
1769	By the above we see that $H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})$
1770	is surjective and hence $H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$.
1771	Since $\mathcal{F}$ was an arbitrary $\mathcal{O}_X$-module with
1772	vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1773	we conclude that also $H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0$ since $\mathcal{Q}$ is
1774	another of these sheaves (see above). By the long exact sequence this in
1775	turn implies that $H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$. And so on and so forth.
1776	\end{proof}
1777

---
name: stacks-cohomology

metadata:

---

# Stacks Project — Cohomology (cohomology.tex)

## Citation
The Stacks Project Authors, "Cohomology", in *Stacks Project*, 2024.
https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/chapter/cohomology

## Slug
stacks-cohomology

## Retrieval status
RETRIEVED — 2026-06-05

## Local source files
- `references/stacks-cohomology.tex` — LaTeX source, 14535 lines, VERIFIED (opens as valid TeX with `\input{preamble}` header and `\begin{document}` — not HTML) — retrieved from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/cohomology.tex

## Why this source
The blueprint chapter `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex` cites two results from the
Stacks "Cohomology" chapter (not "Cohomology of Schemes", which is already local as
`stacks-coherent.tex`):
- `lemma-cech-vanish-basis`: Čech-to-cohomology comparison via a basis (three conditions).
- `lemma-describe-higher-direct-images`: $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheafification of
  $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})$.

## Contents map

Section structure (line numbers in `stacks-cohomology.tex`):

| Line | Section |
|------|---------|
| 17   | Introduction |
| 32   | Cohomology of sheaves |
| 92   | Derived functors |
| 223  | First cohomology and torsors |
| 344  | First cohomology and extensions |
| 398  | First cohomology and invertible sheaves |
| 488  | **Locality of cohomology** ← `lemma-describe-higher-direct-images` |
| 704  | Mayer-Vietoris |
| 868  | The Čech complex and Čech cohomology |
| 1011 | Čech cohomology as a functor on presheaves |
| 1404 | **Čech cohomology and cohomology** ← `lemma-cech-vanish-basis` |
| 1881 | Flasque sheaves |
| 2088 | The Leray spectral sequence |
| 2327 | Functoriality of cohomology |
| 2438 | Refinements and Čech cohomology |
| 2636 | Cohomology on Hausdorff quasi-compact spaces |
| 2955 | The base change map |
| 3050 | Proper base change in topology |
| 3170 | Cohomology and colimits |
| 3439 | Vanishing on Noetherian topological spaces |
| 3739 | Cohomology with support in a closed subset |
| 3857 | Cohomology on spectral spaces |
| 4071 | The alternating Čech complex |
| 4564 | Alternative view of the Čech complex |
| 4742 | Čech cohomology of complexes |
| 5792 | Flat resolutions |
| 6308 | Derived pullback |
| 6545 | Cohomology of unbounded complexes |
| 7156 | Cup product |
| 7813 | Some properties of K-injective complexes |

### Directive-specific lemma locations (deep map)

| Label | Line | Section | Likely Stacks tag (from directive seeds) |
|-------|------|---------|------------------------------------------|
| `lemma-describe-higher-direct-images` | 592 | §Locality of cohomology (l.488) | 01XJ |
| `lemma-cech-vanish-basis` | 1696 | §Čech cohomology and cohomology (l.1404) | 01EO |

**`lemma-describe-higher-direct-images` (line 592):**
Full lemma block: lines 591–603 (`\begin{lemma}` … `\end{lemma}`).
Statement reads: Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces, $\mathcal{F}$ an
$\mathcal{O}_X$-module. The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves associated to
the presheaves $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})$ with restriction mappings as in
Equation (cohomology-equation-restriction-mapping). (Proof: lines 605–627.)

**`lemma-cech-vanish-basis` (line 1696):**
Full lemma block: lines 1695–1714 (`\begin{lemma}` … `\end{lemma}`).
Statement reads: Let $X$ be a ringed space, $\mathcal{B}$ a basis, $\mathcal{F}$ an
$\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Given a set of open coverings $\text{Cov}$ satisfying:
(1) every $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$ has $U, U_i, U_{i_0\ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}$;
(2) for every $U \in \mathcal{B}$ the coverings from $\text{Cov}$ are cofinal;
(3) $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$ and all
$\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$;
then $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$ and $U \in \mathcal{B}$.
(Proof: lines 1716–1776.)

## Caveats
- The source file uses chapter-local label names (e.g. `\label{lemma-cech-vanish-basis}`
  without the `cohomology-` prefix seen in cross-chapter references). When referencing
  from another chapter in the Stacks Project, labels are prefixed with the chapter tag
  (`cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis`).
- The Stacks tag numbers given above (01EO, 01XJ) are from the directive seeds; they
  have not been independently fetched and verified against the live tag server.
- "Cohomology of Schemes" (`coherent.tex`) is already local as `stacks-coherent.tex` —
  do not re-fetch.

## Quality / provenance
This is the authoritative verbatim source downloaded directly from the Stacks Project
GitHub repository (master branch, 2026-06-05). The Stacks Project is the definitive
reference for this material in algebraic geometry.
